verses.text: And it was told Joab, Behold, the king weepeth and mourneth for Absalom. And the victory that day was turned into mourning unto all the people: for the people heard say that day how the king was grieved for his son. And the people gat them by stealth that day into the city, as people being ashamed steal away when they flee in battle. But the king covered his face, and the king cried with a loud voice, O my son Absalom, O Absalom, my son, my son! Guidance: Word of David's continuing lament reaches Joab. The army's morale collapses. 'The victory that day was turned into mourning unto all the people' — what should have been the celebration of a hard-won battle becomes a funeral. The soldiers, sensing the king's grief, slip back into Mahanaim 'by stealth' — the Hebrew implies a furtive return, like guilty defeated troops, even though they have just won decisively. The chapter is showing us the catastrophic effect of a leader's grief publicly displayed in a victory moment. The men who risked their lives to save the kingdom are being made to feel that their efforts produced only sorrow. Meanwhile David covers his face and cries again, audibly. The lament-words from 18:33 are repeated. The chapter is letting us understand why Joab is about to confront the king harshly: somebody has to break the king's grief-loop before it dissolves the army's loyalty.

And Joab came into the house to the king, and said, Thou hast shamed this day the faces of all thy servants, which this day have saved thy life, and the lives of thy sons and of thy daughters, and the lives of thy wives, and the lives of thy concubines; In that thou lovest thine enemies, and hatest thy friends. For thou hast declared this day, that thou regardest neither princes nor servants: for this day I perceive, that if Absalom had lived, and all we had died this day, then it had pleased thee well. Now therefore arise, go forth, and speak comfortably unto thy servants: for I swear by the LORD, if thou go not forth, there will not tarry one with thee this night: and that will be worse unto thee than all the evil that befell thee from thy youth until now. Guidance: Joab's confrontation is one of the harshest speeches by a subordinate to a king in scripture, and one of the most necessary. He lists what the army accomplished: 'saved thy life, and the lives of thy sons and of thy daughters, and the lives of thy wives, and the lives of thy concubines.' The whole royal household survived because these men fought. Then the indictment: 'Thou hast shamed this day the faces of all thy servants… in that thou lovest thine enemies, and hatest thy friends.' Joab names the inversion. David's tears for Absalom signal that, in the king's emotional ledger, the rebel-son matters more than the soldiers who saved the dynasty. The accusation is harsh and partly unfair (David's grief is normal paternal grief, not preference for Absalom over the army), but it is also strategically correct (public mourning for a rebel commander does demoralize loyalist troops). Joab pushes harder: 'If Absalom had lived, and all we had died this day, then it had pleased thee well.' He puts the worst possible interpretation on David's grief to force action. Then the ultimatum: 'If thou go not forth, there will not tarry one with thee this night.' If David does not come out, sit in the gate, and publicly thank the army, the entire force will abandon him by morning. The chapter is showing us a senior officer telling the king truths the king cannot say to himself. Joab is morally compromised (he just disobeyed the king's order and killed Absalom), but here he is also clear-eyed about what the kingdom requires from the king right now. Sometimes the necessary word comes from the most flawed subordinate. David, despite the harshness, will obey.

Then the king arose, and sat in the gate. And they told unto all the people, saying, Behold, the king doth sit in the gate. And all the people came before the king: for Israel had fled every man to his tent. Guidance: David obeys Joab. He rises from his upper chamber, walks down to the city gate, and sits there in public — the standard kingly posture for receiving the army. Word spreads through Mahanaim: 'The king doth sit in the gate.' The army assembles. The funeral is over. The king is back at his job. The verse's last clause shifts focus: 'For Israel had fled every man to his tent.' The northern Israelites — Absalom's supporters — have dispersed back to their homes. The country has no king; David has not yet returned to Jerusalem. The political vacuum is the chapter's next problem. David has resumed his throne in Mahanaim, but a thousand miles of detail are required to actually return him to Jerusalem and re-knit the broken kingdom. The chapter is going to walk us through them.

And all the people were at strife throughout all the tribes of Israel, saying, The king saved us out of the hand of our enemies, and he delivered us out of the hand of the Philistines; and now he is fled out of the land for Absalom. And Absalom, whom we anointed over us, is dead in battle. Now therefore why speak ye not a word of bringing the king back? Guidance: The northern tribes' deliberations. 'All the people were at strife throughout all the tribes of Israel' — the tribes are arguing. Their summary captures the political weight: 'The king saved us out of the hand of our enemies… he delivered us out of the hand of the Philistines; and now he is fled… and Absalom, whom we anointed over us, is dead.' They name the contradiction. David's track record as deliverer (Philistines, Edomites, Ammonites — chapter 8) is irrefutable. Absalom was the alternative they had backed, but he is dead. There is no other king-candidate. 'Why speak ye not a word of bringing the king back?' The northern tribes are reaching the obvious conclusion — restore David — but no one wants to be the first to say it publicly because public commitment to David means accepting personal responsibility for the rebellion they supported. The chapter is showing us the political awkwardness of a population that backed the wrong side and now needs to come back to the right side without losing face. They are looking for a way to invite the king back that does not require them to admit they had supported deposing him.

And king David sent to Zadok and to Abiathar the priests, saying, Speak unto the elders of Judah, saying, Why are ye the last to bring the king back to his house? seeing the speech of all Israel is come to the king, even to his house. Ye are my brethren, ye are my bones and my flesh: wherefore then are ye the last to bring back the king? Guidance: David moves preemptively. He uses Zadok and Abiathar (his priestly intelligence-relay from chapter 15) to send word to the elders of Judah — his own tribe. The message: 'Why are ye the last to bring the king back?' The accusation is shrewd. The northern tribes are publicly debating restoration; David's own tribe of Judah has not yet officially declared. David is shaming Judah for being slower than the rest of the country to invite him home. 'Ye are my brethren, ye are my bones and my flesh' — the kinship appeal. The political move is calculated. David wants Judah to lead the restoration, not Israel. If Judah formally invites him first, the political center of the restoration is in his own tribal home, and the unified kingdom comes back with Judah at its center. The chapter is showing us a king who, even from exile, is shaping his return to maximize his tribe's standing. The strategy is going to produce the very inter-tribal quarrel the chapter ends with.

And say ye to Amasa, Art thou not of my bone, and of my flesh? God do so to me, and more also, if thou be not captain of the host before me continually in the room of Joab. Guidance: David's most controversial decision in the chapter. He sends a private message to Amasa — the very commander Absalom appointed as his army chief (17:25). 'Art thou not of my bone, and of my flesh?' Amasa is David's nephew (Amasa's mother Abigail is David's sister; Joab's mother Zeruiah is also David's sister; the two commanders are cousins). David offers him Joab's job. 'God do so to me, and more also, if thou be not captain of the host before me continually in the room of Joab.' Joab is being demoted. The political logic is layered. (1) Punishment of Joab: David is going to use the kingdom's command structure to discipline the commander who killed Absalom against the king's orders. (2) Reconciliation with the rebel army: Amasa is the rebellion's commander; absorbing him into David's command immediately neutralizes any continuing rebel resistance. The Absalom-supporters know their commander is now David's commander; there is no continuation. (3) Political signal: David is willing to grant senior positions to the defeated side, encouraging the northerners to view the restoration as inclusive rather than vengeful. The decision is going to backfire spectacularly. Joab will kill Amasa in chapter 20 with the same kind of treacherous embrace he used on Abner in chapter 3. The chapter is showing us a king making a politically brilliant move that he does not have the institutional strength to enforce — Joab will simply not accept the demotion.

And he bowed the heart of all the men of Judah, even as the heart of one man; so that they sent this word unto the king, Return thou, and all thy servants. So the king returned, and came to Jordan. And Judah came to Gilgal, to go to meet the king, to conduct the king over Jordan. Guidance: David's message lands. 'He bowed the heart of all the men of Judah, even as the heart of one man.' Judah unites in formal invitation: 'Return thou, and all thy servants.' David crosses west from Mahanaim, reaches the Jordan; Judah's representatives come to Gilgal — the same place where Israel had first crossed the Jordan under Joshua, the most symbolically loaded site for a national restoration. The chapter is showing us the political theater. David is being received by his own tribe at the historic founding-spot of the nation, in a re-enactment that echoes the entry into the Promised Land. The Davidic kingdom is being formally restored. The political theology is exact: the king who fled barefoot weeping over the Mount of Olives is returning across the Jordan, by Gilgal, in solemn procession. The LORD who once delivered Israel through the Jordan is delivering Israel's king back through the same river.

And Shimei the son of Gera, a Benjamite, which was of Bahurim, hasted and came down with the men of Judah to meet king David. And there were a thousand men of Benjamin with him, and Ziba the servant of the house of Saul, and his fifteen sons and his twenty servants with him; and they went over Jordan before the king. And there went over a ferry boat to carry over the king's household, and to do what he thought good. And Shimei the son of Gera fell down before the king, as he was come over Jordan; And said unto the king, Let not my lord impute iniquity unto me, neither do thou remember that which thy servant did perversely the day that my lord the king went out of Jerusalem, that the king should take it to his heart. For thy servant doth know that I have sinned: therefore, behold, I am come the first this day of all the house of Joseph to go down to meet my lord the king. Guidance: Shimei — the Benjamite who cursed David and threw stones at him in chapter 16 — comes to the river-crossing to ask for mercy. He brings a delegation of one thousand Benjamites, presumably to communicate the political weight of his apology (a thousand Benjamites is a major faction; Shimei is leading what remains of the Saulide-loyalist political bloc back into David's coalition). Ziba (the slanderer from 16:1-4) and his fifteen sons and twenty servants are also there, doing logistical service. The 'ferry boat' carries the royal household across — apparently a flat-bottomed barge. Shimei prostrates and apologizes. 'Let not my lord impute iniquity unto me, neither do thou remember that which thy servant did perversely the day that my lord the king went out of Jerusalem.' He does not deny the curse; he asks David to forget it. 'For thy servant doth know that I have sinned: therefore, behold, I am come the first this day of all the house of Joseph to go down to meet my lord the king.' He invokes priority — he is the first Benjamite at the river. The political calculation: Shimei knows that being the first to apologize buys clemency. The chapter is showing us a man whose curse-mouth was emptied in chapter 16 now becoming an apology-mouth. Whether the repentance is genuine or self-protective is unclear; the next confrontation between David and Abishai about whether to kill Shimei is going to test what David will do.

And Shimei the son of Gera fell down before the king, as he was come over Jordan; And said unto the king, Let not my lord impute iniquity unto me, neither do thou remember that which thy servant did perversely the day that my lord the king went out of Jerusalem, that the king should take it to his heart. For thy servant doth know that I have sinned: therefore, behold, I am come the first this day of all the house of Joseph to go down to meet my lord the king. Guidance: Shimei — the Benjamite who cursed David and threw stones at him in chapter 16 — comes to the river-crossing to ask for mercy. He brings a delegation of one thousand Benjamites, presumably to communicate the political weight of his apology (a thousand Benjamites is a major faction; Shimei is leading what remains of the Saulide-loyalist political bloc back into David's coalition). Ziba (the slanderer from 16:1-4) and his fifteen sons and twenty servants are also there, doing logistical service. The 'ferry boat' carries the royal household across — apparently a flat-bottomed barge. Shimei prostrates and apologizes. 'Let not my lord impute iniquity unto me, neither do thou remember that which thy servant did perversely the day that my lord the king went out of Jerusalem.' He does not deny the curse; he asks David to forget it. 'For thy servant doth know that I have sinned: therefore, behold, I am come the first this day of all the house of Joseph to go down to meet my lord the king.' He invokes priority — he is the first Benjamite at the river. The political calculation: Shimei knows that being the first to apologize buys clemency. The chapter is showing us a man whose curse-mouth was emptied in chapter 16 now becoming an apology-mouth. Whether the repentance is genuine or self-protective is unclear; the next confrontation between David and Abishai about whether to kill Shimei is going to test what David will do.

But Abishai the son of Zeruiah answered and said, Shall not Shimei be put to death for this, because he cursed the LORD's anointed? And David said, What have I to do with you, ye sons of Zeruiah, that ye should this day be adversaries unto me? shall there any man be put to death this day in Israel? for do not I know that I am this day king over Israel? Therefore the king said unto Shimei, Thou shalt not die. And the king sware unto him. Guidance: Abishai — the same reaction as in chapter 16 (where he wanted to behead Shimei mid-curse). 'Shall not Shimei be put to death for this, because he cursed the LORD's anointed?' Legally Abishai is correct; cursing the king was a capital offense (Exod 22:28). David refuses. 'What have I to do with you, ye sons of Zeruiah, that ye should this day be adversaries unto me?' The same rebuke he used about Abner in chapter 3 — the sons of Zeruiah are again pushing for blood that David is not willing to shed. 'Shall there any man be put to death this day in Israel?' The argument: today is restoration day; today no Israelite blood is shed. 'Do not I know that I am this day king over Israel?' The kingship-day rationale: a king beginning to reign should not begin with executions. David's mercy is policy, not just personal. 'Thou shalt not die. And the king sware unto him.' David takes an oath. (The oath will haunt the kingdom; David will hand the unresolved Shimei case to Solomon in 1 Kings 2:8-9 with instructions to find a pretext, and Solomon will eventually execute Shimei. David honors his oath in his lifetime; he leaves the legal weight for the next generation.) The chapter is showing us the politics of restoration mercy: clemency now produces national peace; deferred justice can be handled later. Whether the deferred-justice principle is morally satisfactory is left for the reader to wrestle with.

And Mephibosheth the son of Saul came down to meet the king, and had neither dressed his feet, nor trimmed his beard, nor washed his clothes, from the day the king departed until the day he came again in peace. And it came to pass, when he was come to Jerusalem to meet the king, that the king said unto him, Wherefore wentest not thou with me, Mephibosheth? Guidance: Mephibosheth appears. His appearance tells the truth Ziba had hidden. 'He had neither dressed his feet, nor trimmed his beard, nor washed his clothes, from the day the king departed until the day he came again in peace.' He has been in continuous mourning since the day David left — refusing the standard hygiene practices that signal one is at peace. Hebrew kingship-mourning included unwashed feet, untrimmed beard, dirty clothes; Mephibosheth has lived for months in this state. His body is the testimony Ziba could not produce. David asks the bare question: 'Wherefore wentest not thou with me, Mephibosheth?' Why didn't you flee with me? Note the king did not assume from Ziba's slander (16:3) that Mephibosheth was guilty; David is still willing to hear Mephibosheth's account. The chapter is showing us that even after a slander has been accepted in a moment of crisis, the slandered party may still get a hearing later. Mephibosheth is going to use his hearing.

His appearance tells the truth Ziba had hidden. 'He had neither dressed his feet, nor trimmed his beard, nor washed his clothes, from the day the king departed until the day he came again in peace.' David asks the bare question: 'Wherefore wentest not thou with me, Mephibosheth?' Why didn't you flee with me? The chapter is showing us that even after a slander has been accepted in a moment of crisis, the slandered party may still get a hearing later. Mephibosheth is going to use his hearing.

And he answered, My lord, O king, my servant deceived me: for thy servant said, I will saddle me an ass, that I may ride thereon, and go to the king; because thy servant is lame. And he hath slandered thy servant unto my lord the king; but my lord the king is as an angel of God: do therefore what is good in thine eyes. For all of my father's house were but dead men before my lord the king: yet didst thou set thy servant among them that did eat at thine own table. What right therefore have I yet to cry any more unto the king? Guidance: Mephibosheth's defense. 'My servant deceived me' — Ziba. 'I will saddle me an ass, that I may ride thereon, and go to the king; because thy servant is lame.' Mephibosheth had planned to ride out with David; he asked Ziba to saddle the donkey he needed because of his disability. Ziba, instead of saddling for his master, took the donkeys himself and went to David with the slander. Mephibosheth, left behind without transportation, could not catch up. He spent the rebellion at home, mourning. 'He hath slandered thy servant.' Direct accusation. Then the gracious self-deprecation: 'My lord the king is as an angel of God: do therefore what is good in thine eyes.' He puts the verdict in David's hands. Then the most beautiful self-disqualification: 'All of my father's house were but dead men before my lord the king: yet didst thou set thy servant among them that did eat at thine own table. What right therefore have I yet to cry any more unto the king?' Mephibosheth invokes the chapter-9 covenant kindness. By the laws of dynastic succession, the surviving male heirs of Saul should have been executed when David took the throne. David's kindness in 2 Sam 9 saved Mephibosheth's life and gave him a place at the royal table — a gift Mephibosheth has not forgotten. 'What right have I yet to cry?' He waives any further claim. The chapter is showing us a victim of slander who, given the chance to be defended, defends himself with humility rather than indignation. He names the slanderer; he denies the charge; he reaffirms gratitude for the kindness shown to him before. There is no political ambition in his speech; only the desire to be known accurately to the king he had not betrayed.

And the king said unto him, Why speakest thou any more of thy matters? I have said, Thou and Ziba divide the land. And Mephibosheth said unto the king, Yea, let him take all, forasmuch as my lord the king is come again in peace unto his own house. Guidance: David's verdict is a compromise. 'Why speakest thou any more of thy matters? I have said, Thou and Ziba divide the land.' He splits the estate fifty-fifty between Mephibosheth and Ziba. The decision is widely criticized by commentators. The right call would have been to restore the full estate to Mephibosheth and punish Ziba for slander; the wrong call would have been to leave Ziba in full possession. David's split is an attempt to avoid revisiting his earlier rushed decision (16:4) while not fully crediting Mephibosheth's defense. It is, frankly, a king tired of court intrigue settling a case half-heartedly. The chapter is showing us a David who, in the press of restoration politics, does not give a slander-victim the full justice he deserves. Mephibosheth's response is its own moral verdict: 'Yea, let him take all, forasmuch as my lord the king is come again in peace unto his own house.' Mephibosheth says he doesn't care about the estate; let Ziba have it all; the joy of the king's safe return is what matters. The verse is one of the gentlest in 2 Samuel. The slandered prince refuses to fight for his property; the king's restoration is enough for him. The chapter is showing us moral clarity in a marginal figure that exceeds the moral clarity of the king himself. Sometimes the most faithful person in the room is the one who waives his rights to keep the peace.

And Barzillai the Gileadite came down from Rogelim, and went over Jordan with the king, to conduct him over Jordan. Now Barzillai was a very aged man, even fourscore years old: and he had provided the king of sustenance while he lay at Mahanaim; for he was a very great man. Guidance: Barzillai — the Gileadite landowner from Rogelim who had been one of the three patrons providing supplies at Mahanaim (17:27) — comes down to escort David across the Jordan. The narrator gives us his profile: 'a very aged man, even fourscore years old' (eighty); 'a very great man' (rich, influential, regionally respected). He has provided for David's army through the months of exile. He has come to walk the king to the river as a final gesture of honor. The chapter is going to dwell on Barzillai's farewell because his choice — to refuse court honors in favor of dying at home — is the most morally beautiful refusal of royal patronage in the Old Testament.

And Barzillai the Gileadite came down from Rogelim, and went over Jordan with the king, to conduct him over Jordan. Now Barzillai was a very aged man, even fourscore years old: and he had provided the king of sustenance while he lay at Mahanaim; for he was a very great man. Guidance: Barzillai — the Gileadite landowner from Rogelim who had been one of the three patrons providing supplies at Mahanaim (17:27) — comes down to escort David across the Jordan. The narrator gives us his profile: 'a very aged man, even fourscore years old' (eighty); 'a very great man' (rich, influential, regionally respected). He has provided for David's army through the months of exile. He has come to walk the king to the river as a final gesture of honor. The chapter is going to dwell on Barzillai's farewell because his choice — to refuse court honors in favor of dying at home — is the most morally beautiful refusal of royal patronage in the Old Testament.

And the king said unto Barzillai, Come thou over with me, and I will feed thee with me in Jerusalem. And Barzillai said unto the king, How long have I to live, that I should go up with the king unto Jerusalem? I am this day fourscore years old: and can I discern between good and evil? can thy servant taste what I eat or what I drink? can I hear any more the voice of singing men and singing women? wherefore then should thy servant be yet a burden unto my lord the king? Thy servant will go a little way over Jordan with the king: and why should the king recompense it me with such a reward? Let thy servant, I pray thee, turn back again, that I may die in mine own city, and be buried by the grave of my father and of my mother. But behold thy servant Chimham; let him go over with my lord the king; and do to him what shall seem good unto thee. Guidance: Barzillai's refusal is one of the most graceful in scripture. David offers him court provision in Jerusalem — the standard royal reward for a major patron. Barzillai declines, with one of the most realistic descriptions of advanced age in the Old Testament. (1) 'How long have I to live?' Realistic life-expectancy assessment. He is eighty; the move would not pay off in years of enjoyment. (2) 'Can I discern between good and evil?' His mental faculties are declining; the political life of a court requires moral judgment he no longer has the sharpness for. (3) 'Can thy servant taste what I eat or what I drink?' Taste buds dulled. (4) 'Can I hear any more the voice of singing men and singing women?' Hearing loss. Court life depends on appreciating its pleasures; Barzillai has lost the sensory capacity to enjoy them. (5) 'Wherefore then should thy servant be yet a burden unto my lord the king?' The senior statesman thinking about not being a charity case at the royal table. The argument is dignified pragmatism. (6) 'Let thy servant, I pray thee, turn back again, that I may die in mine own city, and be buried by the grave of my father and of my mother.' The deepest motive: home and ancestral burial. Barzillai wants to die where he has lived, beside his parents. (7) Then the gesture: 'But behold thy servant Chimham; let him go over with my lord the king.' Chimham is probably Barzillai's son; sending him to court means Barzillai's family gets the royal patronage without Barzillai having to relocate. The chapter is showing us a man who, having served the king at the critical moment, has the self-knowledge to decline the king's reward. There is a category of grace in declining honors that we have outlived our capacity to enjoy. Barzillai is the model. The chapter also models how to substitute family-membership for personal-membership in royal life. Barzillai's name and influence will continue through Chimham at court; Barzillai himself will go home.

David offers him court provision in Jerusalem — the standard royal reward for a major patron. Barzillai declines, with one of the most realistic descriptions of advanced age in the Old Testament. (1) 'How long have I to live?' Realistic life-expectancy assessment. He is eighty; the move would not pay off in years of enjoyment. (2) 'Can I discern between good and evil?' His mental faculties are declining; the political life of a court requires moral judgment he no longer has the sharpness for. (3) 'Can thy servant taste what I eat or what I drink?' Taste buds dulled. (4) 'Can I hear any more the voice of singing men and singing women?' Hearing loss. Court life depends on appreciating its pleasures; Barzillai has lost the sensory capacity to enjoy them. (5) 'Wherefore then should thy servant be yet a burden unto my lord the king?' The senior statesman thinking about not being a charity case at the royal table. The argument is dignified pragmatism. (6) 'Let thy servant, I pray thee, turn back again, that I may die in mine own city, and be buried by the grave of my father and of my mother.' The deepest motive: home and ancestral burial. Barzillai wants to die where he has lived, beside his parents. (7) Then the gesture: 'But behold thy servant Chimham; let him go over with my lord the king.' Chimham is probably Barzillai's son; sending him to court means Barzillai's family gets the royal patronage without Barzillai having to relocate. The chapter is showing us a man who, having served the king at the critical moment, has the self-knowledge to decline the king's reward. There is a category of grace in declining honors that we have outlived our capacity to enjoy. Barzillai is the model. The chapter also models how to substitute family-membership for personal-membership in royal life. Barzillai's name and influence will continue through Chimham at court; Barzillai himself will go home.

And the king said unto Barzillai, Come thou over with me, and I will feed thee with me in Jerusalem. And Barzillai said unto the king, How long have I to live, that I should go up with the king unto Jerusalem? I am this day fourscore years old: and can I discern between good and evil? can thy servant taste what I eat or what I drink? can I hear any more the voice of singing men and singing women? wherefore then should thy servant be yet a burden unto my lord the king? Thy servant will go a little way over Jordan with the king: and why should the king recompense it me with such a reward? Let thy servant, I pray thee, turn back again, that I may die in mine own city, and be buried by the grave of my father and of my mother. But behold thy servant Chimham; let him go over with my lord the king; and do to him what shall seem good unto thee. Guidance: Barzillai's refusal is one of the most graceful in scripture. David offers him court provision in Jerusalem — the standard royal reward for a major patron. Barzillai declines, with one of the most realistic descriptions of advanced age in the Old Testament. (1) 'How long have I to live?' Realistic life-expectancy assessment. He is eighty; the move would not pay off in years of enjoyment. (2) 'Can I discern between good and evil?' His mental faculties are declining; the political life of a court requires moral judgment he no longer has the sharpness for. (3) 'Can thy servant taste what I eat or what I drink?' Taste buds dulled. (4) 'Can I hear any more the voice of singing men and singing women?' Hearing loss. Court life depends on appreciating its pleasures; Barzillai has lost the sensory capacity to enjoy them. (5) 'Wherefore then should thy servant be yet a burden unto my lord the king?' The senior statesman thinking about not being a charity case at the royal table. The argument is dignified pragmatism. (6) 'Let thy servant, I pray thee, turn back again, that I may die in mine own city, and be buried by the grave of my father and of my mother.' The deepest motive: home and ancestral burial. Barzillai wants to die where he has lived, beside his parents. (7) Then the gesture: 'But behold thy servant Chimham; let him go over with my lord the king.' Chimham is probably Barzillai's son; sending him to court means Barzillai's family gets the royal patronage without Barzillai having to relocate. The chapter is showing us a man who, having served the king at the critical moment, has the self-knowledge to decline the king's reward. There is a category of grace in declining honors that we have outlived our capacity to enjoy. Barzillai is the model. The chapter also models how to substitute family-membership for personal-membership in royal life. Barzillai's name and influence will continue through Chimham at court; Barzillai himself will go home.

And the king answered, Chimham shall go over with me, and I will do to him that which shall seem good unto thee: and whatsoever thou shalt require of me, that will I do for thee. And all the people went over Jordan. And when the king was come over, the king kissed Barzillai, and blessed him; and he returned unto his own place. Guidance: David accepts Barzillai's terms. Chimham will go to Jerusalem; David promises Barzillai whatever he asks. The royal household crosses the Jordan. At the crossing, David kisses Barzillai farewell and blesses him. Barzillai returns home. The chapter is showing us the model dignified farewell. The aged patron has done what he came to do (escort the king across), accepts the king's honor (the kiss, the blessing), substitutes his son for himself in the long-term arrangement, and goes home to die. Centuries later, Jeremiah 41:17 will record an inn called 'the habitation of Chimham' near Bethlehem — a property David apparently granted Chimham, which the descendants of Barzillai held for generations. (Some traditions read this as the very inn where Jesus would later be born — the Bethlehem inn-stable-area belonging to the descendants of David's friend.) The chapter is letting us see that small acts of patronage to the right king at the right time can echo across millennia.

And all the people went over Jordan. And when the king was come over, the king kissed Barzillai, and blessed him; and he returned unto his own place. Guidance: David accepts Barzillai's terms. Barzillai returns home. The chapter is showing us the model dignified farewell. Centuries later, Jeremiah 41:17 will record an inn called 'the habitation of Chimham' near Bethlehem — a property David apparently granted Chimham, which the descendants of Barzillai held for generations. (Some traditions read this as the very inn where Jesus would later be born — the Bethlehem inn-stable-area belonging to the descendants of David's friend.) The chapter is letting us see that small acts of patronage to the right king at the right time can echo across millennia.

Then the king went on to Gilgal, and Chimham went on with him: and all the people of Judah conducted the king, and also half the people of Israel. And, behold, all the men of Israel came to the king, and said unto the king, Why have our brethren the men of Judah stolen thee away, and have brought the king, and his household, and all David's men with him, over Jordan? Guidance: The chapter's last quarrel. David proceeds with Chimham toward Gilgal. The Judahite delegation is escorting him; half the northern Israelites also come. Then the rest of the northern delegation catches up — and they are angry. 'Why have our brethren the men of Judah stolen thee away, and have brought the king, and his household, and all David's men with him, over Jordan?' The complaint: Judah moved faster than Israel to bring the king back, and the Israelites feel cut out of the restoration ceremony. The political question is real — the formal escort of the king back to Jerusalem is the constitutional moment of his re-acclamation; the tribe(s) that performs the escort get the political honor. Judah, having moved first (per v14), has captured the honor at Israel's expense. The chapter is showing us the inter-tribal jealousy that will eventually split the kingdom under Rehoboam (1 Kgs 12). The unified kingdom David built is, in this verse, already cracking along the line between Judah and the northern tribes.

Then the king went on to Gilgal, and Chimham went on with him: and all the people of Judah conducted the king, and also half the people of Israel. And, behold, all the men of Israel came to the king, and said unto the king, Why have our brethren the men of Judah stolen thee away, and have brought the king, and his household, and all David's men with him, over Jordan? Guidance: The chapter's last quarrel. David proceeds with Chimham toward Gilgal. The Judahite delegation is escorting him; half the northern Israelites also come. Then the rest of the northern delegation catches up — and they are angry. 'Why have our brethren the men of Judah stolen thee away, and have brought the king, and his household, and all David's men with him, over Jordan?' The complaint: Judah moved faster than Israel to bring the king back, and the Israelites feel cut out of the restoration ceremony. The political question is real — the formal escort of the king back to Jerusalem is the constitutional moment of his re-acclamation; the tribe(s) that performs the escort get the political honor. Judah, having moved first (per v14), has captured the honor at Israel's expense. The chapter is showing us the inter-tribal jealousy that will eventually split the kingdom under Rehoboam (1 Kgs 12). The unified kingdom David built is, in this verse, already cracking along the line between Judah and the northern tribes.

And all the men of Judah answered the men of Israel, Because the king is near of kin to us: wherefore then be ye angry for this matter? have we eaten at all of the king's cost? or hath he given us any gift? And the men of Israel answered the men of Judah, and said, We have ten parts in the king, and we have also more right in David than ye: why then did ye despise us, that our advice should not be first had in bringing back our king? And the words of the men of Judah were fiercer than the words of the men of Israel. Guidance: The argument escalates. Judah's reply: 'The king is near of kin to us' — David is Judahite; tribal kinship gave Judah the priority. 'Have we eaten at all of the king's cost? or hath he given us any gift?' Judah is not financially benefiting; we just escorted our cousin home. The defense is weak but technically true; David did not pay Judah for the escort. Israel's counter: 'We have ten parts in the king, and we have also more right in David than ye.' The northern tribes have ten of the twelve; mathematically they constitute the larger constituency. 'Why then did ye despise us, that our advice should not be first had in bringing back our king?' We were the larger faction; we should have led the restoration. The chapter's editorial closer: 'The words of the men of Judah were fiercer than the words of the men of Israel.' Judah won the rhetorical fight but only by being more aggressive — not more right. The chapter ends with the unified kingdom restored politically (David back across the Jordan, marching toward Jerusalem) but already fractured tribally. The seeds of the chapter-20 rebellion of Sheba the Benjamite are being planted by Judah-Israel tensions in this verse. The Davidic kingdom is back; the cracks in its foundation are visible. The chapter is teaching us that the costs of restoration include not just the rebellion that preceded it but the inter-group resentment it produces. The wisdom of David's later years will involve learning to hold these tensions together. The chapter does not pretend the restoration is complete; chapter 20 will open with the consequences.
